sect2.title: Introduction to samurai
sect2.para: The samurai package provides a drop-in replacement for the Ninja package. It aims for full compatibility.

note.para: This package works for most packages as a Ninja replacement. It's known to work just fine for 32-bit on 64-bit and with projects with Rust, as well. However, it can be bitten by some build systems with potentially ambiguous dependency information, causing important targets to be skipped that Ninja papers over. If you find that a project tries to include a header like so: #include "xdg-shell.h", but can't, it is probably generated by a target which got skipped. You can try using Ninja to see if that resolves the issue.

listitem.para: (examples are disabled so this issue cannot be observed, but if enabled, then it cannot include "xdg-shell-protocol.h")

sect2.title: Installation of samurai
sect2.para: Install samurai by running the following commands:

sect3.title: Configuration Information
sect3.para: If you want samu to be used whenever ninja is run, make a symlink as the &root; user:

screen.userinput: if [ "$(file /usr/bin/ninja | grep ELF)" ]; then mv -v /usr/bin/ninja{,.old} fi && ln -svf samu /usr/bin/ninja

sect3.para: samu won't run on all logical CPUs or threads by default, similar to make. The process to make it do so is the same: pass -j<x>, where <x> is the number of logical CPUs you want to use whenever samu (or ninja if you made the symlink) is run. You can get around having to specify it every time by including -j<x> in SAMUFLAGS like so:
If MAKEFLAGS only contains the -j option, you can set SAMUFLAGS to MAKEFLAGS like so:
If you'd like to avoid manually setting SAMUFLAGS each time you log in, add it to a Bash profile script like ~/.bash_profile.

listitem.para: is a drop-in replacement for ninja
